questions.text: Does the response focus on the person who made the claim (their character, motive, or past), or does it engage the claim itself?
Does the conclusion rest mainly on something other than reasons (a feeling, a name, a head-count, or a label like 'natural'), or is there evidence and reasoning you could examine?
Does the support point somewhere other than the conclusion (restating the claim, citing timing alone, or making a sweeping leap), or does it actually bear on it?
Is the issue framed as just two paths when a middle ground or other options genuinely exist, or are the available choices presented fairly?
After the response, is the original claim left standing while attention moves to the speaker, or has the claim actually been addressed?
Is a reason to distrust the speaker being offered as if it settles whether their claim is true, or is the claim weighed on its own?
Is the version being argued against a more extreme or absolute version of the claim, or the one actually made?
Would the person who made the claim say the restatement of their position has been changed on a key point, or would they recognize and accept it?
Are the two options presented genuinely the only ones, or is a compromise, a 'both', or a 'neither' realistically available?
Is a workable middle ground or 'do both' option being treated as if it weren't on the table, or are all the realistic positions actually represented?
Are the steps from the first move to the feared outcome treated as certain to follow without support, or shown to be likely?
Is the feared end result much larger and more alarming than the small step being discussed, or close to it in scale?
Is the claim supported mainly by who endorsed it (a respected person, expert, or celebrity), or by reasons you could check?
Is the cited source speaking outside their field while being treated as the whole case, or does their expertise actually fit this topic?
Is a sweeping 'always/everyone' claim resting on one case or a small handful, or does the range of examples match how broad the conclusion is?
Are the examples cherry-picked or anecdotal cases standing in for the whole group, or do they look like a representative range?
Does the support already assume the conclusion is true (the reason and the claim restating each other), or is it an independent reason?
Does accepting the premise require already accepting the conclusion, or would the argument persuade someone who doesn't already agree?
If you set aside the emotional pull (fear, pity, anger), was the feeling carrying the conclusion, or is there still a reason left that supports it?
Is the conclusion pressed mainly by stirring a strong feeling, or by reasons that would still stand if the case were put calmly?
Does the case rest only on the two things happening together or one before the other, or is there a mechanism or evidence for the cause?
Is a correlation read directly as cause, or are other explanations (coincidence, a shared third cause, reverse direction) considered and ruled out?
Is the critic's own conduct or inconsistency being used to set the criticism aside, or is the criticism itself being answered on its merits?
Is the pointed-out inconsistency raised only to deflect the point, or is it genuinely relevant because consistency or a double standard is the actual question?
Is 'natural' (or 'artificial/man-made') being used as the main reason something is good or bad, or is there separate evidence of its actual effects?
Does the argument treat naturalness alone as settling it across the board, or does it check the specific case for benefit or harm?
Is 'natural means good' (or 'man-made means harmful') doing the persuading here, or is the verdict tied to measured benefit or harm in this case?
Is how many people believe or do it offered as the reason it's true or right, or as one clue alongside independent reasons?
Is going along with the crowd treated as the reason to agree, or is independent evidence given beyond 'most people' or 'everyone' agreeing?
Is the choice boxed into exactly two camps when other positions are open, or are the realistic options all on the table?
Is each step from the first move toward the extreme treated as bound to follow, or is the chain shown to be likely at each link?
Does the case lean on the prestige of the name attached to it, or on reasons that would hold without that name?
Does the reason given simply restate the conclusion in other words, or add something the conclusion did not already say?
Is the push toward the conclusion mainly an emotional pull, or a reason that holds once the feeling is set aside?
Is 'one came first, so it caused the other' the whole case, or is a mechanism or ruling-out of other causes also given?
Is being natural (or not man-made) treated as settling that it is good or safe, or is that judged by its measured effects?
